query: recent studies on surgical effort in quaternary cytoreductive surgery for ovarian cancer and its effect on survival

retrieved_docs.title: Cytoreduction (CRS) and Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (HIPEC) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Van Driel et al. published in the New England journal of medicine the results of a multicenter randomized study on the treatment of peritoneal carcinoma in ovarian carcinoma. A statistically significant advantage was clearly demonstrated for the combined treatment compared to cytoreduction alone, especially in improving patient survival. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment by Cancer Type
retrieved_docs.content: EOPPC This is managed similarly to serous ovarian carcinomas, with a multifaceted treatment approach aimed at optimizing patient outcomes. The standard treatment protocol involves surgical intervention followed by chemotherapy and targeted therapy. Surgical procedures typically include hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and omentectomy, which are performed in all cases to remove the primary tumor and any involved tissues. Following surgery, patients receive chemotherapy, often incorporating platinum-based agents as part of a neoadjuvant or adjuvant treatment strategy. However, the emergence of platinum-resistant tumors has necessitated the exploration of alternative treatment modalities. Targeted therapy with poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors has shown promising results in blocking deoxyribonucleic acid repair mechanisms and enhancing treatment efficacy. PARP inhibitors such as olaparib, rucaparib, olaparib, or veliparib are combined with chemotherapy to target specific molecular pathways in cancer growth and progression. Additionally, intraperitoneal chemotherapy has demonstrated superior outcomes to intravenous administration, as evidenced by a phase III clinical trial showing improved overall survival rates. [74] Debulking surgery, CRS, is crucial in achieving optimal outcomes, excising as much tumor tissue as possible, leaving <2 cm residual nodules. CRS is performed with chemotherapy, producing optimal results in 33% to 69% of patients. [1] In cases of tumor recurrence, salvage chemotherapy regimens are employed, which may include agents such as doxorubicin, methotrexate, paclitaxel, and 5-fluorouracil. The combination of surgery, chemotherapy, and targeted therapy offers a comprehensive approach to managing EOPPC, with the potential to improve patient prognosis and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recurrent Ovarian Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Consequently, those with advanced-stage ovarian cancer are generally treated with primary reductive surgery, followed by platinum-based chemotherapy. However, poor surgical candidates or patients who might not achieve effective cytoreductive surgery are recommended to undergo neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Optimal cytoreductive surgery is critical as reduced tumor burden is a powerful predictor of survival. Targeted therapies are the new emerging treatment strategies where bevacizumab and PARP inhibitors have become first-line therapies for maintenance, and PARP inhibitors are preferred for recurrent cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Staging and Primary Debulking Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Ovarian cancer is surgically staged using the FIGO and tumor, node, metastasis (TNM) grading system. (Refer to the Staging section for more information on ovarian cancer staging). Evidence demonstrates that chemotherapy is more effective and improves the overall prognosis in patients with optimal surgical staging. Because primary surgical staging and debulking are so critical to patient outcomes, the procedure should be performed by an experienced gynecologic oncologist in a facility with the necessary equipment available. Surgical staging typically comprises a midline laparotomy with abdominal and pelvic exploration, ascites or peritoneal washing collection, total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, pelvic and paraaortic lymph node evaluation, peritoneal biopsy, and omentectomy. Optimal surgical debulking resects all grossly visible disease to reduce tumor burden to the greatest extent, particularly in epithelial ovarian cancer subtypes less affected by chemotherapy (eg, low-grade serous, clear cell, and mucinous carcinoma). [1] In patients with stage III disease or greater, cytoreduction to less than 1 cm should be performed if complete resection is not possible; more extensive procedures may also be involved, including bowel and diaphragm resection and upper abdominal exploration. Routine pelvic and paraaortic lymphadenectomy is not recommended by the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO), but enlarged lymph nodes should be excised. However, some professional societies (eg, the European Society for Medical Oncology) do recommend systematic pelvic and paraaortic lymphadenectomy for high-grade disease. [1] [37] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Cytoreduction (CRS) and Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (HIPEC) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Primary forms, such as peritoneal mesothelioma and peritoneal carcinoma, must be differentiated from the peritoneal spread of malignant tumors, either gastrointestinal tract or ovarian; a median survival of 6 months for colorectal cancer and 3 months for gastric cancer shows a clearly limited prognosis. Results for peritoneal mesothelioma with systemic chemotherapy are similar. The probability of survival is better for advanced ovarian cancer, for which a median survival of up to 2 years is possible. Treatment should be multimodal and include preoperative systemic chemotherapy, complete cytoreductive surgery, and intraabdominal hyperthermia combined with intraoperative chemotherapy. Thus the complete surgical cytoreduction possible is decisive for the prognosis, which requires multiple organ resections in addition to a partial to subtotal parietal peritonectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recurrent Ovarian Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: About 80% of women with advanced-stage ovarian cancer more commonly have tumor progression or recurrence. Platinum-free interval is one of the most reliable predictors indicating the response of recurrent ovarian cancer to subsequent chemotherapy. The platinum-free interval is the interval between the completion of the last platinum-based chemotherapy and relapse. [57] However, platinum sensitivity generally refers to an interval of more than 6 months between the previous platinum-based chemotherapy cycle and the commencement of subsequent platinum-based chemotherapy. The role of surgery in cases of recurrent ovarian cancer is undefined. GOG-213, a phase 3 multicenter randomized clinical trial enrolled patients with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer, randomized patients to surgical cytoreductive surgery followed by adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy or only platinum-based chemotherapy with a primary endpoint of overall survival showed no improved benefit in patients receiving secondary surgical cytoreduction followed by chemotherapy and chemotherapy alone (hazard ratio for death 1.29, 0.97 to 1.72; P = .08). [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: In advanced-stage ovarian cancer cases, intraperitoneal carboplatin chemotherapy is well-tolerated. Since ovarian cancer growth is initially mainly in the abdominal cavity, intraperitoneal chemotherapy was thought to provide an increased therapeutic benefit by directly diffusing chemotherapeutic agents into cancer tissue, primarily for minimal amounts of remnant disease. Improved survival benefit of intraperitoneal chemotherapy was shown in 4 landmark trials: GOG-104, GOG-114, and GOG-172. However, the GOG-252 trial showed when bevacizumab was combined with intraperitoneal chemotherapy, adverse effects (eg, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, neurotoxicity, and gastrointestinal symptoms) were increased, without any benefit in survival. Therefore, with the recent increase in the number of indications for bevacizumab, intraperitoneal chemotherapy is less utilized. [6] [65] Additional trials are still underway, which may provide additional information on this treatment avenue. [6] [66] [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Anti-angiogenic inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Angiogenesis inhibitors are agents that target tumorous vascular growth mechanisms. Bevacizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody against vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) that has been studied in combination with chemotherapy and was the first anti-angiogenic agent approved in the United States. [43] [46] In 2 major landmark trials (ICON7 and GOG-0218), patients with advanced-stage ovarian cancer had improved progression-free survival in the maintenance bevacizumab cohort when compared with surveillance only. [50] Therefore, bevacizumab for maintenance therapy for stage III or IV epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer in patients with a complete or partial response following primary surgical debulking and bevacizumab in combination with carboplatin and paclitaxel, is recommended by the NCCN. However, if bevacizumab is not used during the primary treatment with chemotherapy, the NCCN does not recommend use for maintenance therapy. Additionally, anti-angiogenic inhibitors are not recommended in patients with BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations because other targeted therapies are more effective. In patients with wild-type or an unknown BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutation status, bevacizumab is still appropriate. [45] Conversely, ASCO is more conservative in its recommendation of bevacizumab. Though bevacizumab significantly benefits progression-free survival when used as concurrent therapy followed by single-agent maintenance therapy, no clear clinical benefit in overall survival has been demonstrated; therefore, ASCO merely suggests that it may be considered until more evidence is found. [37] Other angiogenesis inhibitors include pazopanib, sorafenib, sunitinib, cediranib, and aflibercept; however, these medications are not frequently used due to adverse effects or cost. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Platinum-based chemotherapy agents
retrieved_docs.content: In patients who demonstrated a good response following primary cytoreduction and chemotherapy, clinical discretion previously guided the decision to initiate maintenance therapy as evidence from several studies showed increased toxicity with no significant improved overall survival with its use. The phase 3 randomized trial, GOG-178, evaluated patients diagnosed with stage III to IV ovarian cancer, comparing the outcomes of 2 maintenance therapy durations following complete clinical response to platinum/paclitaxel therapy: 12 months versus 3 months of paclitaxel maintenance therapy. After 50% accrual interval analysis, improved progression-free survival was seen favoring the extended therapy cohort. However, the study closed early. A follow-up study later showed no overall survival benefit compared to the same maintenance monotherapy for 22 months versus 14 months. [47] Another trial, GOG-175, showed no significant difference in 5-year survival or recurrence-free interval where high-risk early-stage ovarian cancer patients were randomized to observational versus weekly paclitaxel 40 mg/m² for 24 weeks after completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin and paclitaxel for 3 cycles. [48] A 3-arm phase 3 trial following standard chemotherapy, GOG-0212, compared observation without immediate therapy to 12 months of paclitaxel or polyglutamated paclitaxel but showed disappointing results. [49] Therefore, targeted therapies that are less toxic have replaced chemotherapy as maintenance therapy agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Treatment Fields in Oncology -- Indications -- Ovarian Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: TTF has also gained popularity in the treatment of recurrent ovarian cancer, and one such trial, INNOVATE, focuses on the safety and feasibility of TTF in combination with paclitaxel.

retrieved_docs.title: Cytoreduction (CRS) and Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (HIPEC) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: According to several clinical studies, infiltration of the abdominal cavity by neoplastic cells is inevitable following surgical manipulation during peritonectomy. The main goal of intraperitoneal chemotherapy is to eliminate these free tumor cells and any micrometastases that are not visible to the naked eye. Classically, HIPEC is performed after surgical resection and before reconstruction of the gastrointestinal tract. This potentially curative therapeutic option completes the concept of multimodal therapy, and it is increasingly needed for patients with peritoneal metastatic gastrointestinal and gynecological tumors and mainly peritoneal malignancies. Several prospective studies have proven the superiority of the concept of multimodal therapy over a single systemic therapy in the treatment of metastatic cancers. [27] [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment by Cancer Type
retrieved_docs.content: Primary leiomyosarcoma of the peritoneal cavity These present as aggressive tumors requiring comprehensive management strategies. Resectable tumors are typically addressed with extensive wide-margin surgeries aimed at complete tumor removal. For metastatic disease, systemic chemotherapy plays a crucial role in controlling tumor progression and improving patient outcomes. Both preoperative and postoperative radiotherapy are considered essential components of treatment to enhance local tumor control and reduce the risk of recurrence. In recent years, CRS and HIPEC have emerged as significant therapeutic approaches for managing peritoneal leiomyosarcomas. This combined modality treatment has shown promising results in improving patient survival and disease control by delivering chemotherapy directly to the peritoneal cavity. Intraperitoneal chemotherapy achieves higher local drug concentrations by bypassing hepatic metabolism and the first-pass effect, leading to more effective tumor eradication and better clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy and Interval Debulking Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Various clinical trials have compared neoadjuvant chemotherapy with interval cytoreduction surgery versus primary cytoreductive surgery. These trials have shown overall survival is the same between patients who underwent primary cytoreduction and those who had neoadjuvant chemotherapy; however, neoadjuvant therapy may reduce complications, including the necessity of bowel resection and stoma formation. [42] Other trials are still investigating which treatment approach is most effective. Therefore, interprofessional consultation, including gynecologic oncologists, should individualize treatment decisions based on clinical factors when determining the optimal treatment approach. [6] [43] In patients who do undergo neoadjuvant chemotherapy, the tumor is reassessed with imaging to determine resectability after 3 to 4 cycles of chemotherapy. [6] [43] If tumor progression is observed during neoadjuvant chemotherapy, interval debulking surgery is not recommended, as studies have not demonstrated any survival benefit. Enrollment in clinical trials or treatment cessation and beginning end-of-life care may be considered in these patients. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Adjuvant Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Following primary debulking surgery, adjuvant chemotherapy decisions should be guided by surgical staging as disease characterization may change following surgical assessment, eg, upstaging patients thought to have early-stage disease. Stage I grade 3, stage IC and II, high-grade, and clear cell ovarian cancers are at high risk for recurrence; stage IA or IB grade 1 endometrioid, serous, or mucinous ovarian cancers are at low risk for recurrence. In low-grade early-stage disease, adjuvant chemotherapy does not improve overall survival. Therefore, though optimal primary cytoreduction alone may treat low-risk early-stage ovarian cancers, high-risk early-stage disease benefits from adjuvant chemotherapy; furthermore, stage I ovarian cancers that are inadequately staged or suboptimally resected should also be administered adjuvant chemotherapy if additional surgery is unable to be performed. A combination of carboplatin and paclitaxel are the most commonly used agents, though recommendations regarding agents, dosage, or duration have not been established. For advanced-stage disease, chemotherapy regimens, including the administration mode, are determined based on the presence of residual disease. (Refer to the Medical Oncology section for more information on chemotherapy). [22] [37] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Ovarian cancer, despite ongoing clinical trials and advancements in treatment, remains a significant challenge in women's health due to late-stage diagnosis and deviation from recommended care guidelines. Efforts are needed to develop effective strategies for early detection and optimize treatment outcomes. Experienced gynecologic oncologists are critical in achieving optimal cytoreduction surgery, a key determinant of patient survival. Interprofessional collaboration, particularly between medical and surgical oncologists, facilitates shared decision-making regarding treatment options and enrollment in clinical trials. Pathologists provide essential diagnostic information through tissue biopsies, guiding treatment decisions. Early involvement in palliative care improves treatment efficacy and enhances patients' quality of life. Close surveillance and patient education on symptom recognition for disease recurrence are vital for long-term management. With its complex nature and diverse treatment modalities, ovarian cancer care necessitates a patient-centered approach, integrating the expertise of physicians, advanced practitioners, nurses, pharmacists, and other health professionals to improve patient outcomes, safety, and team performance.

retrieved_docs.title: Cytoreduction (CRS) and Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (HIPEC) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The oncologist must respect the correct selection of patients in agreement with the surgeon to perform this procedure by taking into account the beneficial effects of HIPEC on the long-term survival of the oncological patient without forgetting the benefit/risk ratio.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2760
retrieved_docs.content: إزالة المبيض جزئيًا (partial oophorectomy) and استئصال الرحم جزئيًا (partial hysterectomy) are gynecologic surgical procedures that frequently co‑occur, often being performed together in the management of conditions requiring simultaneous partial removal of ovarian and uterine tissue. This co‑occurrence reflects a clinical approach where both organs are addressed in a single operative session to treat disease, reduce patient morbidity, and streamline postoperative recovery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Partial removal of the ovary (إزالة المبيض جزئيًا) and partial removal of the uterus (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا) are surgical procedures that frequently co‑occur in clinical practice. Similarly, bilateral removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries (استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي) also commonly co‑occurs with partial hysterectomy (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا). The repeated association of these gynecologic surgeries indicates that, when extensive pelvic pathology or prophylactic indications are present, clinicians often combine ovarian or salpingo‑oophorectomy with a partial hysterectomy to address multiple reproductive organs in a single operative session, thereby streamlining patient care and reducing the need for separate procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Salpingo‑oophorectomy, complete or partial, unilateral or bilateral (separate procedure) is closely related to oophorectomy; unilateral and bilateral, as both surgical interventions involve removal of ovarian tissue and may be performed on one side (unilateral) or both sides (bilateral).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1891
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage of ovarian cyst by abdominal approach and Salpingo‑oophorotomy are both classified as other operations on ovary, indicating that each procedure is a specific type of ovarian surgery within a broader category of ovarian operative interventions. These two techniques represent distinct surgical approaches used to treat ovarian pathology, yet they share the common relationship of being subprocedures under the umbrella term “other operations on ovary,” highlighting their relevance to the overall management of ovarian conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882
retrieved_docs.content: Partial salpingo‑oophorectomy is frequently performed together with partial hysterectomy (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا). Likewise, partial removal of the ovary (إزالة المبيض جزئيًا) co‑occurs with partial hysterectomy, and bilateral salpingo‑oophorectomy (استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي) is also commonly combined with partial hysterectomy. The repeated co‑occurrence of these procedures indicates that surgeons often address uterine and adnexal pathology in a single operative session, which can be advantageous for conditions such as extensive fibroids, endometriosis, or gynecologic malignancies by providing comprehensive treatment and reducing the need for multiple separate surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1890
retrieved_docs.content: Radical trachelectomy with removal of left ovary is a surgical procedure whose primary focus is the excision of the left ovary (استئصال المبيض الأيسر). The operation of removing the left fallopian tube and ovary (استئصال البوق والمبيض في جهة اليسار) is classified as a specific type of left‑ovary removal (استئصال المبيض الأيسر). Both descriptions underscore that the removal of the left ovary is a central component of these gynecologic surgeries, which may be performed for oncologic management or fertility‑preserving treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2092
retrieved_docs.content: of both ovaries and the uterus (استئصال المبيضين والرحم), reflecting the spectrum of surgical options from organ‑preserving to radical excision. Overall, these interrelated procedures illustrate a structured taxonomy of uterine, cervical, and ovarian surgeries, their co‑occurrence patterns, and their inclusion in a unified classification of malignant‑disease‑related therapeutic and diagnostic interventions, which is essential for accurate coding, clinical decision‑making, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2092
retrieved_docs.content: (unilateral and bilateral) is identified as a type of ovary removal (استئصال المبيض) and frequently co‑occurs with abdominal hysterectomy, appendectomy (استئصال الزائدة), and bilateral oophorectomy procedures. This oophorectomy category is a member of the “الإجراءات العلاجية والتشخيصية ذات الصلة بالأمراض الخبيثة (SMQ),” the set of therapeutic and diagnostic interventions associated with malignant diseases, as are abdominal hysterectomy and vaginal hysterectomy. The procedure “total hysterectomy with unilateral removal of ovary” specifically focuses on the ovary removal component, underscoring the clinical importance of preserving or removing a single ovary in certain oncologic contexts. Additionally, a direct relationship is noted between ovary removal (استئصال المبيض) and the combined removal of both ovaries and the uterus (استئصال المبيضين والرحم), reflecting the spectrum of surgical options from organ‑preserving to radical excision. Overall, these interrelated procedures illustrate

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2823
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians specializing in Obstetrics & Gynecology are trained to perform salpingo‑oophorectomy procedures, which may involve complete or partial removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries and can be conducted unilaterally or bilaterally as a separate surgical operation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Radical trachelectomy with removal of right ovary is a combined surgical approach in which the primary focus is the excision of the right ovary, referred to in Arabic as استئصال المبيض الأيمن.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2845
retrieved_docs.content: The female reproductive system encompasses surgical interventions such as abdominal hysterectomy, a procedure in which the uterus is removed through an abdominal incision. Abdominal hysterectomy frequently co‑occurs with salpingo‑oophorectomy, the removal of the fallopian tube and ovary, indicating that these gynecologic surgeries are often performed together. This combined approach is commonly employed in the management of conditions affecting the uterus and adnexal structures, such as malignant or benign tumors, severe endometriosis, or other complex pelvic diseases, and informs surgical planning and patient counseling in women’s health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 520
retrieved_docs.content: of ovarian cysts (إزالة تكيسات المبيض), salpingo‑oophorectomy, appendectomy (استئصال الزائدة), and ovariectomy. Ablative techniques are linked both to “hysterectomy; abdominal and vaginal” and to appendectomy, indicating their use in these surgical contexts. Oncologic associations are highlighted by frequent pairing of abdominal hysterectomy with high‑grade cervical neoplasia, specifically cervical cancer in situ (CIN III) and other CIN grades, underscoring the role of abdominal hysterectomy as a therapeutic or prophylactic intervention for severe cervical disease. Finally, total hysterectomy with unilateral tube removal is a specialized variant of complete abdominal hysterectomy, illustrating the hierarchical taxonomy of female reproductive surgeries and the typical procedural combinations that guide surgical planning and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2616
retrieved_docs.content: Partial removal of the ovary (إزالة المبيض جزئيًا) and partial hysterectomy (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا) are frequently performed together, reflecting a combined surgical strategy. Similarly, bilateral salpingo‑oophorectomy (استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي) often co‑occurs with partial hysterectomy, indicating that extensive removal of the fallopian tubes, ovaries, and part of the uterus is a common approach in the treatment of certain gynecologic malignancies. Both partial hysterectomy and vaginal hysterectomy (استئصال الرحم عن طريق المهبل) are classified as members of the SMQ group “Therapeutic and diagnostic procedures related to malignant diseases” (الإجراءات العلاجية والتشخيصية ذات الصلة بالأمراض الخبيثة), underscoring their role in oncologic care. The relationships among these procedures highlight how multiple gynecologic surgeries are coordinated to achieve comprehensive management of malignant disease and are grouped together for coding and reporting purposes in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2797
retrieved_docs.content: إزالة تكيسات المبيض (removal of ovarian cysts) frequently co‑occurs with استئصال قناة فالوب (salpingectomy), indicating that these two gynecologic procedures are often performed together in clinical practice. Both Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral and Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, unilateral are specific forms of استئصال قناة فالوب, representing bilateral and unilateral laparoscopic approaches to partial removal of the fallopian tubes. The association of ovarian cyst removal with either bilateral or unilateral laparoscopic partial salpingectomy underscores the common surgical strategy of addressing ovarian pathology alongside targeted fallopian tube resection when indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1601
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure described as “total hysterectomy with unilateral removal of ovary” is centered on the surgical step of استئصال المبيض (ovary removal). Within the hierarchy of ovarian surgeries, إزالة المبيض جزئيًا (partial ovary removal) is identified as a child or sub‑type of استئصال المبيض, indicating that it represents a less extensive form of the same organ’s excision. A more specific and extensive form, استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي (bilateral salpingo‑oophorectomy), is characterized as having a narrower relationship to استئصال المبيض, meaning it is a precise subset that involves removal of both fallopian tubes and ovaries. Moreover, استئصال المبيض is a component of the broader combined operation استئصال المبيضين والرحم (removal of both ovaries and the uterus), linking the single‑organ procedure to a more extensive gynecologic resection. Clinical practice patterns show that إزالة المبيض جزئيًا frequently co‑occurs with استئصال الرحم جزئيًا (partial hysterectomy), and similarly, استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي also tends to be performed alongside استئصال الرحم جزئيًا. These relationships illustrate a structured hierarchy of ovarian surgical techniques and their common association with partial uterine removal, informing surgical planning and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 845
retrieved_docs.content: techniques (e.g., drainage of ovarian cysts, salpingo‑oophorotomy, partial salpingo‑oophorectomy, various forms of tubal ligation) are organized under intermediate categories (عملية المبيض, other operations on ovary, other operations on fallopian tubes) and higher‑level groupings (Upper female genital tract operation). This hierarchical organization clarifies how specific gynecologic procedures relate to one another, aiding clinicians in categorizing interventions, understanding their scope, and planning comprehensive patient care.
